name: Bagsværd Church
writeup: Bagsværd Church sits quietly in a Copenhagen suburb, its corrugated concrete exterior giving almost no indication of what lies within. The interior is dominated by a continuous shell-like ceiling of cast concrete that billows and folds like clouds, flooding the nave with diffuse northern light. Utzon described the design as a collision between the universal — the cloud ceiling derived from ancient Chinese pavilion forms — and the local, the modest Danish parish. It is one of the most spatially surprising rooms in twentieth century architecture, and one of the least visited.
detail: The undulating ceiling is formed from prefabricated concrete shell segments that overlap and interlock, each piece slightly different, creating an effect of movement despite being entirely static.
